front: What is the primary difference between Memory-Mapped I/O and Isolated I/O (Port-Mapped I/O)?
back: Memory-Mapped I/O uses the same address space for both memory and I/O devices, so I/O devices are accessed like memory locations. Isolated I/O uses a separate, dedicated address space for I/O devices and requires special instructions (e.g., IN, OUT) to access them.

front: What are the three primary methods for performing I/O data transfers?
back: 1. Programmed I/O (Polling): The CPU repeatedly checks the status of an I/O device. 2. Interrupt-Driven I/O: The I/O device interrupts the CPU when it is ready to transfer data. 3. Direct Memory Access (DMA): A dedicated controller (DMAC) manages the data transfer directly between the I/O device and main memory, freeing the CPU.

front: What is the key advantage of using an Interrupt-Driven I/O scheme over Programmed I/O (Polling)?
back: It greatly improves CPU efficiency. Instead of the CPU wasting time constantly checking (polling) the status of a device, it can execute other tasks. The I/O device itself sends a signal (an interrupt) only when it needs service, allowing the CPU to respond promptly.

front: What is the role of a DMA (Direct Memory Access) Controller?
back: The DMA controller is a specialized hardware unit that takes over the task of transferring large blocks of data between an I/O device and main memory. It operates independently of the CPU, which only needs to initiate the transfer, thus freeing the CPU to perform other tasks and avoiding cycle-stealing from the program.

front: What is an I/O Interface (or I/O Controller)?
back: It is a hardware circuit that sits between the system bus and an I/O device. It provides the necessary buffering, timing, and signal conversion to allow the CPU to communicate with a device that may have different data formats, speeds, and voltage levels. Examples include a USB controller or a disk drive controller.
